preamble: The phonetic transformation from the voiced labio-velar approximant /w/ to the alveolar lateral approximant /l/ is a less common sound change compared to its inverse, L-vocalization. However, it is attested in specific linguistic contexts, involving a shift in the place of articulation from the labio-velar region to the alveolar ridge, and a change from a central approximant to a lateral approximant.
phoneticEffects: This shift involves delabialization (loss of lip rounding and labial constriction) and a fronting of the articulation from the velar region to the alveolar region, resulting in lateral airflow

examples.note: Shift occurred in the environment of low vowels /a/ and /ö/.

sources: Merrill, W. L. (2013). The Genetic Unity of Southern Uto-Aztecan. Language Dynamics and Change, 3(1), 68-104.
